clinical_coherence.notes: 62M African American with HTN, DM2, OSA, LVH, and prior anterior STEMI with PCI. Presenting with recurrent syncope and near-syncope. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) -- trifascicular disease pattern. Brief convulsive movements witnessed, creating seizure vs syncope differential. Prior misdiagnosis as seizures by outside physician adds clinical realism.

tool_output_consistency.notes: ECG shows bifascicular block with first-degree AV block (trifascicular pattern). Holter captures three episodes of Mobitz type II with pauses up to 5.8 seconds. EEG normal. Labs show mild hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia from thiazide (contributing to conduction risk). EP study shows HV interval 82ms confirming severe infra-Hisian disease. All outputs concordant.

ground_truth_quality.notes: R55 ICD code acceptable. Trifascicular disease with intermittent Mobitz type II correctly identified. EP study with prolonged HV interval as confirmatory test. Critical actions include electrolyte correction and discontinuing thiazide.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Moderate difficulty well-calibrated. The baseline ECG does not show complete heart block (only bifascicular block), requiring Holter to capture intermittent Mobitz type II. Convulsive syncope mimicking seizure adds diagnostic complexity. EP study needed for definitive diagnosis.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 62M African American with extensive cardiovascular history, prior STEMI, and multiple medications is realistic. HCTZ contributing to electrolyte depletion and conduction risk is clinically relevant.

educational_value.notes: Outstanding teaching case. Demonstrates trifascicular disease, intermittent Mobitz type II requiring Holter capture, convulsive syncope vs epileptic seizure, thiazide-induced electrolyte effects on conduction, and the role of EP study with HV interval measurement.

strengths: Intermittent conduction disease requiring Holter for capture, unlike the straightforward CHB cases
Convulsive syncope as seizure mimic is clinically important
EP study with HV interval measurement adds diagnostic depth
Thiazide-induced electrolyte effects on conduction system
